---
title: "SIR model using SymPy"
author: "Jong-Hoon Kim"
date: "2023-10-16"
categories: [SIR, SymPy]
image: "SimPy_logo.svg.png"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/vancouver
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, echo=TRUE) 
```

I attempted to replicate some of the simple analytical results presented in *Mathematical Epidemiology* [@brauer2008]. The SIR model equations below follow the classical formulation of Kermack and McKendrick [@kermack1927].

$$
\begin{align}
\mathrm{d}S/\mathrm{d}t &= -\beta I S \\
\mathrm{d}I/\mathrm{d}t &= \beta I S - \gamma I\\
\end{align}
$$ The first part is simply to compute $dI/dS$.

```{python}
from sympy import *

R_0, b, g, dIdt, dSdt, S, I = symbols('R_0 b g dIdt dSdt S I')

dSdt = - b*S*I
dIdt = + b*S*I - g*I
dSdI = dIdt / dSdt #-(I*S*b - I*g)/(I*S*b)

# b <- R0*g
simplify(-(I*S*R_0*g - I*g)/(I*S*R_0*g)) 
```

The second part is to integrate the equation $\text{d}I/\text{d}S$, which yields the relationship between $S$ and $I$ along a trajectory — the SIR orbit equation. This result is equivalent to $I(S) = I_0 + S_0 - S + (1/R_0)\ln(S/S_0)$ described in @hethcote2000.

```{python}
S, I = symbols("S I", cls=Function)
b, g, t, R_0, S0, I0 = symbols("b g t R_0 S0 I0")

eq = Eq(I(t).diff(t), - S(t).diff(t) + (1/R_0)*(1/S(t))*S(t).diff(t))

integrate(eq, t)
```

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
